content.text:
Provide a code review for the given pull request.

To do this, follow these steps precisely:

1. Use a Haiku agent to check if the pull request (a) is closed, (b) is a draft, (c) does not need a code review (eg. because it is an automated pull request, or is very simple and obviously ok), or (d) already has a code review from you from earlier. If so, do not proceed.
2. Use another Haiku agent to give you a list of file paths to (but not the contents of) any relevant CLAUDE.md files from the codebase: the root CLAUDE.md file (if one exists), as well as any CLAUDE.md files in the directories whose files the pull request modified
3. Use a Haiku agent to view the pull request, and ask the agent to return a summary of the change
4. Then, launch 5 parallel Sonnet agents to independently code review the change. The agents should do the following, then return a list of issues and the reason each issue was flagged (eg. CLAUDE.md adherence, bug, historical git context, etc.):
   a. Agent #1: Audit the changes to make sure they compily with the CLAUDE.md. Note that CLAUDE.md is guidance for Claude as it writes code, so not all instructions will be applicable during code review.
   b. Agent #2: Read the file changes in the pull request, then do a shallow scan for obvious bugs. Avoid reading extra context beyond the changes, focusing just on the changes themselves. Focus on large bugs, and avoid small issues and nitpicks. Ignore likely false positives.
   c. Agent #3: Read the git blame and history of the code modified, to identify any bugs in light of that historical context
   d. Agent #4: Read previous pull requests that touched these files, and check for any comments on those pull requests that may also apply to the current pull request.
   e. Agent #5: Read code comments in the modified files, and make sure the changes in the pull request comply with any guidance in the comments.
5. For each issue found in #4, launch a parallel Haiku agent that takes the PR, issue description, and list of CLAUDE.md files (from step 2), and returns a score to indicate the agent's level of confidence for whether the issue is real or false positive. To do that, the agent should score each issue on a scale from 0-100, indicating its level of confidence. For issues that were flagged due to CLAUDE.md instructions, the agent should double check that the CLAUDE.md actually calls out that issue specifically. The scale is (give this rubric to the agent verbatim):
   a. 0: Not confident at all. This is a false positive that doesn't stand up to light scrutiny, or is a pre-existing issue.
   b. 25: Somewhat confident. This might be a real issue, but may also be a false positive. The agent wasn't able to verify that it's a real issue. If the issue is stylistic, it is one that was not explicitly called out in the relevant CLAUDE.md.
   c. 50: Moderately confident. The agent was able to verify this is a real issue, but it might be a nitpick or not happen very often in practice. Relative to the rest of the PR, it's not very important.
   d. 75: Highly confident. The agent double checked the issue, and verified that it is very likely it is a real issue that will be hit in practice. The existing approach in the PR is insufficient. The issue is very important and will directly impact the code's functionality, or it is an issue that is directly mentioned in the relevant CLAUDE.md.
   e. 100: Absolutely certain. The agent double checked the issue, and confirmed that it is definitely a real issue, that will happen frequently in practice. The evidence directly confirms this.
6. Filter out any issues with a score less than 80. If there are no issues that meet this criteria, do not proceed.
7. Use a Haiku agent to repeat the eligibility check from #1, to make sure that the pull request is still eligible for code review.
8. Finally, use the gh bash command to comment back on the pull request with the result. When writing your comment, keep in mind to:
   a. Keep your output brief
   b. Avoid emojis
   c. Link and cite relevant code, files, and URLs

Examples of false positives, for steps 4 and 5:

- Pre-existing issues
- Something that looks like a bug but is not actually a bug
- Pedantic nitpicks that a senior engineer wouldn't call out
- Issues that a linter, typechecker, or compiler would catch (eg. missing or incorrect imports, type errors, broken tests, formatting issues, pedantic style issues like newlines). No need to run these build steps yourself -- it is safe to assume that they will be run separately as part of CI.
- General code quality issues (eg. lack of test coverage, general security issues, poor documentation), unless explicitly required in CLAUDE.md
- Issues that are called out in CLAUDE.md, but explicitly silenced in the code (eg. due to a lint ignore comment)
- Changes in functionality that are likely intentional or are directly related to the broader change
- Real issues, but on lines that the user did not modify in their pull request

Notes:

- Do not check build signal or attempt to build or typecheck the app. These will run separately, and are not relevant to your code review.
- Use `gh` to interact with Github (eg. to fetch a pull request, or to create inline comments), rather than web fetch
- Make a todo list first
- You must cite and link each bug (eg. if referring to a CLAUDE.md, you must link it)
- For your final comment, follow the following format precisely (assuming for this example that you found 3 issues):

---

### Code review

Found 3 issues:

1. <brief description of bug> (CLAUDE.md says "<...>")

<link to file and line with full sha1 + line range for context, note that you MUST provide the full sha and not use bash here, eg. https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/1d54823877c4de72b2316a64032a54afc404e619/README.md#L13-L17>

2. <brief description of bug> (some/other/CLAUDE.md says "<...>")

<link to file and line with full sha1 + line range for context>

3. <brief description of bug> (bug due to <file and code snippet>)

<link to file and line with full sha1 + line range for context>

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code)

<sub>- If this code review was useful, please react with 👍. Otherwise, react with 👎.</sub>

---

- Or, if you found no issues:

---

### Code review

No issues found. Checked for bugs and CLAUDE.md compliance.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code)

- When linking to code, follow the following format precisely, otherwise the Markdown preview won't render correctly: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-cli-internal/blob/c21d3c10bc8e898b7ac1a2d745bdc9bc4e423afe/package.json#L10-L15
  - Requires full git sha
  - You must provide the full sha. Commands like `https://github.com/owner/repo/blob/$(git rev-parse HEAD)/foo/bar` will not work, since your comment will be directly rendered in Markdown.
  - Repo name must match the repo you're code reviewing
  - # sign after the file name
  - Line range format is L[start]-L[end]
  - Provide at least 1 line of context before and after, centered on the line you are commenting about (eg. if you are commenting about lines 5-6, you should link to `L4-7`)

1→# 🔍 Browser Compare
     2→
     3→A CLI tool to capture and compare screenshots of any webpage across multiple mobile browsers, devices, and in-app browser environments. Built with Playwright, which ships real Chromium, WebKit, and Firefox engines.
     4→
     5→## Quick Start
     6→
     7→```bash
     8→# 1. Install dependencies
     9→npm install
    10→
    11→# 2. Install browser engines (downloads ~400MB of browser binaries)
    12→npx playwright install
    13→
    14→# 3. Capture screenshots
    15→node capture.mjs https://your-site.com
    16→
    17→# 4. Open the report
    18→open report/index.html
    19→```
    20→
    21→## Usage Examples
    22→
    23→### Basic — all browsers, default devices
    24→```bash
    25→node capture.mjs https://example.com
    26→```
    27→
    28→### Compare specific browsers and devices
    29→```bash
    30→node capture.mjs https://example.com \
    31→  --browsers chromium,webkit \
    32→  --devices "iPhone 15 Pro,Pixel 7,iPad Pro 11"
    33→```
    34→
    35→### Test in-app browsers (Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, etc.)
    36→```bash
    37→node capture.mjs https://example.com \
    38→  --ua-spoof facebook,instagram,tiktok,wechat,line,twitter,snapchat
    39→```
    40→
    41→### Full comparison — everything
    42→```bash
    43→node capture.mjs https://example.com \
    44→  --browsers chromium,webkit,firefox \
    45→  --ua-spoof facebook,instagram,tiktok,wechat,line,twitter,snapchat \
    46→  --delay 3000 \
    47→  --output ./full-report
    48→```
    49→
    50→### Custom viewport
    51→```bash
    52→node capture.mjs https://example.com --width 390 --height 844
    53→```
    54→
    55→## CLI Options
    56→
    57→| Option | Default | Description |
    58→|--------|---------|-------------|
    59→| `--output, -o` | `./report` | Output directory for report and screenshots |
    60→| `--browsers` | `chromium,webkit,firefox` | Comma-separated browser engines |
    61→| `--devices` | See below | Comma-separated Playwright device names |
    62→| `--ua-spoof` | none | In-app browser UA spoofing (see list below) |
    63→| `--delay` | `2000` | Wait ms after page load before screenshot |
    64→| `--full-page` | `true` | Capture full scrollable page |
    65→| `--width, -w` | device default | Custom viewport width |
    66→| `--height, -h` | device default | Custom viewport height |
    67→| `--inject-eruda` | `false` | Inject Eruda debug console |
    68→
    69→## Default Devices
    70→
    71→- iPhone 15 Pro
    72→- iPhone 13
    73→- iPhone SE (3rd gen)
    74→- Pixel 7
    75→- Galaxy S9+
    76→- iPad Pro 11
    77→
    78→## In-App Browser UA Spoofing
    79→
    80→| Key | Simulates |
    81→|-----|-----------|
    82→| `facebook` | Facebook iOS + Android in-app browser |
    83→| `instagram` | Instagram iOS + Android in-app browser |
    84→| `tiktok` | TikTok iOS + Android in-app browser |
    85→| `wechat` | WeChat in-app browser |
    86→| `line` | LINE in-app browser |
    87→| `twitter` | Twitter/X iOS in-app browser |
    88→| `snapchat` | Snapchat in-app browser |
    89→
    90→Using `--ua-spoof facebook` will test both `facebook_ios` and `facebook_android` variants.
    91→
    92→## Report Features
    93→
    94→The generated HTML report (`report/index.html`) includes:
    95→
    96→- **Grid View** — see all screenshots at once
    97→- **Side-by-Side View** — horizontally scroll through comparisons
    98→- **Compare Mode** — select 2 screenshots for direct comparison
    99→- **Filter by Engine** — Chromium / WebKit / Firefox / In-App
   100→- **Filter by Platform** — iOS / Android
   101→- **Click to Zoom** — lightbox for full-size inspection
   102→- **Stats Dashboard** — overview of test coverage
   103→
   104→## How It Works
   105→
   106→1. **Playwright** ships with real browser engines (Chromium, WebKit, Firefox) — not simulations
   107→2. For each browser + device combo, it creates a context with the device's viewport, pixel ratio, and user agent
   108→3. For in-app browsers, it uses the real engine that the in-app browser uses (WebKit for iOS, Chromium for Android) but spoofs the user agent string to trigger any UA-specific behavior
   109→4. Screenshots are captured after page load + configurable delay
   110→5. An HTML report is generated for visual comparison
   111→
   112→## Using with Claude Code
   113→
   114→This tool is designed to work great with Claude Code:
   115→
   116→```bash
   117→# Ask Claude Code to test your site
   118→claude "Use browser-compare to test https://my-site.com across all major browsers and in-app browsers, then analyze the differences"
   119→
   120→# Or for a specific issue
   121→claude "I think my site's header looks broken on Safari. Use browser-compare to capture screenshots on WebKit vs Chromium for iPhone 15 Pro and show me the differences"
   122→```
   123→
   124→## Important Notes
   125→
   126→- **WebKit ≈ Safari**: Playwright's WebKit engine closely mirrors Safari's rendering, making it the best option for Safari testing outside of a real Mac/iPhone.
   127→- **In-app UA spoofing**: This spoofs the user agent string but uses the same underlying engine. Real in-app browsers may have additional restrictions (blocked APIs, injected scripts, etc.) that can't be fully simulated.
   128→- **For complete in-app testing**: Use the report to catch rendering issues, but also test on real devices for API/feature restrictions.
   129→
   130→## Limitations
   131→
   132→| What it captures well | What it can't fully simulate |
   133→|----------------------|----------------------------|
   134→| CSS rendering differences across engines | In-app browser JavaScript restrictions |
   135→| Viewport/safe area behavior | Injected tracking scripts (Meta Pixel, etc.) |
   136→| Font rendering differences | Cookie isolation / OAuth flow issues |
   137→| Layout and responsive behavior | File upload / download restrictions |
   138→| UA-dependent CSS/JS behavior | Navigation/back button behavior |
   139→
   140→## License
   141→
   142→MIT
   143→

1→# Mobile Browser Compatibility Testing Tool — Project Context
     2→
     3→## Project Goal
     4→
     5→Build a developer tool that captures and compares how a target webpage renders across different mobile browser environments — including Safari, Chrome, Firefox, and in-app browsers (Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, WeChat, LINE, etc.) — then presents the results in a visual comparison report to identify display differences.
     6→
     7→## Why This Matters
     8→
     9→Web pages render differently across mobile browsers due to engine differences, platform restrictions, and in-app browser limitations. Developers currently have no easy way to see all these differences at a glance. This tool solves that.
    10→
    11→---
    12→
    13→## Core Architecture
    14→
    15→### Tech Stack
    16→- **Playwright** — ships real Chromium, WebKit (≈Safari), and Firefox engines. This is the foundation.
    17→- **Node.js** (ESM) — CLI tool
    18→- **HTML/CSS/JS** — comparison report viewer (self-contained, no build step)
    19→
    20→### How It Works
    21→1. User provides a target URL and configuration options
    22→2. Tool launches each browser engine via Playwright
    23→3. For each browser + device combo, it creates a context with correct viewport, pixel ratio, and user agent
    24→4. For in-app browsers, it uses the matching engine (WebKit for iOS, Chromium for Android) with spoofed user agent strings
    25→5. Screenshots are captured after page load + configurable delay
    26→6. An HTML report is generated for side-by-side visual comparison
    27→
    28→### Key Files (starter code already exists)
    29→- `capture.mjs` — main CLI entry point, handles screenshot capture across all browser/device/UA combos
    30→- `report-template.html` — the HTML comparison report with grid/side-by-side/diff views, filtering, lightbox
    31→- `package.json` — project config
    32→- `README.md` — usage documentation
    33→
    34→---
    35→
    36→## Critical Domain Knowledge
    37→
    38→### Rendering Engine Landscape
    39→
    40→| Browser | iOS Engine | Android Engine |
    41→|---------|-----------|----------------|
    42→| Safari | WebKit | N/A |
    43→| Chrome | WebKit (forced by Apple) | Chromium/Blink |
    44→| Firefox | WebKit (forced by Apple) | Gecko |
    45→| Facebook In-App | WKWebView (WebKit) | Android WebView (Chromium) |
    46→| Instagram In-App | WKWebView (WebKit) | Android WebView (Chromium) |
    47→| TikTok In-App | WKWebView (WebKit) | Android WebView (Chromium) |
    48→| WeChat In-App | WKWebView (WebKit) | Tencent X5 Engine |
    49→| LINE In-App | WKWebView (WebKit) | Android WebView (Chromium) |
    50→
    51→**Key insight**: On iOS, ALL browsers use WebKit. So the real engine-level rendering differences are WebKit vs Blink vs Gecko. In-app browser issues are more about restricted APIs, injected scripts, viewport behavior, and cookie isolation than rendering engine differences.
    52→
    53→### Major CSS/JS Compatibility Issues to Test For
    54→
    55→1. **`100vh` bug** — Safari's 100vh includes the URL bar, causing content to overflow. Fix: `100dvh`.
    56→2. **Safe area insets** — iPhone notch/Dynamic Island requires `viewport-fit=cover` + `env(safe-area-inset-*)`.
    57→3. **Input auto-zoom** — Safari zooms page when focusing inputs with `font-size < 16px`.
    58→4. **`-webkit-backdrop-filter`** — Safari needs the `-webkit-` prefix.
    59→5. **Flexbox `gap`** — Broken on Safari < 14.1.
    60→6. **Date parsing** — `new Date("2024-01-15 10:00:00")` fails on Safari (needs `T` separator).
    61→7. **`position: sticky`** — Breaks inside overflow containers on Safari.
    62→8. **Font rendering** — Safari uses Core Text, Chrome uses HarfBuzz/Skia — causes different text metrics and line wrapping.
    63→
    64→### In-App Browser Specific Problems
    65→
    66→| Issue | Affected Browsers | Impact |
    67→|-------|------------------|--------|
    68→| JS injection (tracking scripts) | Facebook, Instagram, TikTok | Can conflict with page JS |
    69→| Cookie/storage isolation | All in-app browsers | OAuth/auth flows break |
    70→| `localStorage` cleared between sessions | Facebook, Instagram | State persistence fails |
    71→| `window.open()` blocked | All in-app browsers | Popups don't work |
    72→| File download fails silently | Most in-app browsers | PDF/file downloads broken |
    73→| Reduced viewport height (header bar) | All in-app browsers | Layout overflow |
    74→| Deep links unreliable | Facebook, Instagram | App links may not trigger |
    75→| Camera/mic (WebRTC) blocked | TikTok, some others | Media features unavailable |
    76→| Swipe gestures conflict | Instagram, TikTok | Scroll/swipe UI breaks |
    77→
    78→### In-App Browser User Agent Strings
    79→
    80→These are the real UA strings used to detect each in-app browser:
    81→
    82→- **Facebook**: Contains `FBAN` or `FBAV`
    83→- **Instagram**: Contains `Instagram`
    84→- **TikTok**: Contains `BytedanceWebview` or `TikTok`
    85→- **WeChat**: Contains `MicroMessenger`
    86→- **LINE**: Contains `Line/`
    87→- **Twitter/X**: Contains `Twitter`
    88→- **Snapchat**: Contains `Snapchat`
    89→
    90→---
    91→
    92→## Report UI Features (already implemented in starter)
    93→
    94→- **Grid View** — all screenshots in a responsive grid
    95→- **Side-by-Side View** — horizontal scroll for direct comparison
    96→- **Compare Mode** — select exactly 2 for diff
    97→- **Filter by Engine** — Chromium / WebKit / Firefox / In-App
    98→- **Filter by Platform** — iOS / Android
    99→- **Click-to-Zoom Lightbox** — full-size inspection
   100→- **Stats Dashboard** — test coverage overview
   101→
   102→---
   103→
   104→## What's Already Built vs What Needs Work
   105→
   106→### ✅ Already built (starter code)
   107→- Core capture logic in `capture.mjs` (CLI args, Playwright launch, screenshot loop, in-app UA spoofing)
   108→- HTML report template with all view modes and filtering
   109→- 10 in-app browser UA profiles
   110→- 6 default device profiles
   111→- Report generation with metadata JSON
   112→
   113→### 🔧 Needs improvement / extension
   114→- **Error handling** — more robust error handling, retries for flaky page loads
   115→- **Parallel capture** — currently sequential; could parallelize across browsers
   116→- **CSS diff highlighting** — pixel-diff two screenshots and highlight differences (e.g., using `pixelmatch` or `sharp`)
   117→- **Automated compatibility tests** — inject JS test snippets to check feature support per browser and include results in report
   118→- **Config file support** — allow a `.browsercompare.json` config file instead of CLI args only
   119→- **Watch mode** — re-capture on file change for development workflows
   120→- **CI integration** — output machine-readable results (JSON) for CI pipelines, fail on visual regression thresholds
   121→- **Interactive test page** — a built-in HTML page with known problematic patterns (100vh, sticky, backdrop-filter, date parsing, etc.) that can be used as a reference test target
   122→- **Open in browser button detection** — detect in-app browser and suggest opening in system browser
   123→
   124→---
   125→
   126→## Device Profiles Available in Playwright
   127→
   128→The starter uses these defaults, but Playwright has many more:
   129→- iPhone 15 Pro, iPhone 13, iPhone SE (3rd gen)
   130→- Pixel 7, Galaxy S9+
   131→- iPad Pro 11
   132→
   133→Full list: `npx playwright devices` or see Playwright docs.
   134→
   135→---
   136→
   137→## File Structure Target
   138→
   139→```
   140→browser-compare/
   141→├── capture.mjs              # Main CLI tool
   142→├── report-template.html     # HTML report template
   143→├── package.json
   144→├── README.md
   145→├── .browsercompare.json     # Optional config file (TODO)
   146→├── lib/
   147→│   ├── browsers.mjs         # Browser/device/UA profile definitions
   148→│   ├── capture-engine.mjs   # Core capture logic
   149→│   ├── diff.mjs             # Pixel diff engine (TODO)
   150→│   ├── report-generator.mjs # Report HTML generation
   151→│   └── feature-tests.mjs    # Injected JS feature detection tests (TODO)
   152→├── test-pages/
   153→│   └── compatibility.html   # Built-in test page with known issues (TODO)
   154→└── report/                  # Generated output (gitignored)
   155→    ├── index.html
   156→    ├── metadata.json
   157→    └── screenshots/
   158→```
   159→

    91→ * @returns {'ios' | 'android' | 'desktop'}
    92→ */
    93→export function getPlatform(device) {
    94→  if (!device.isMobile) return 'desktop';
    95→  return device.defaultBrowserType === 'webkit' ? 'ios' : 'android';
    96→}
    97→

   104→  firefox: { launcher: firefox, label: 'Firefox' },
   105→};

## Complete Codebase Overview: Browser Compatibility Screenshot Comparison Tool

This is a **CLI tool that captures and compares screenshots of webpages across multiple browser engines, devices, and in-app browser environments** using Playwright. It generates an interactive HTML report for visual comparison.

---

### PROJECT PURPOSE

Solve the problem: **"How do websites render across different mobile browsers?"**

The tool helps developers:
- See rendering differences across Safari (WebKit), Chrome (Chromium), Firefox
- Compare desktop vs mobile layouts
- Test in-app browsers (Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, WeChat, LINE, Twitter, Snapchat)
- Identify CSS/JS compatibility issues quickly

---

### OVERALL ARCHITECTURE

```
browser-comp/
├── capture.mjs                    # Main CLI entry point (115 lines)
├── lib/
│   ├── browsers.mjs               # Browser/device/UA profile definitions (105 lines)
│   ├── capture-engine.mjs         # Core screenshot capture logic (200 lines)
│   └── report-generator.mjs       # HTML report generation (102 lines)
├── report-template.html           # Interactive HTML report template (592 lines)
├── browser-compatibility-explorer.jsx  # React component (knowledge base only)
├── package.json                   # Project config (ES modules, Playwright dependency)
├── README.md                       # User documentation
├── CONTEXT.md                      # Project context & requirements
├── CLAUDE.md                       # Development guidelines (hidden in .gitignore)
└── report/                         # Generated output directory (gitignored)
    ├── index.html
    ├── metadata.json
    └── screenshots/
```

---

### CORE FILES & FUNCTIONS

#### 1. **capture.mjs** (115 lines) — CLI Entry Point
**Purpose:** Parse command-line arguments, load config, orchestrate the capture workflow

**Key Functions:**
- `parseArgs(args)` — Parse CLI flags like `--browsers`, `--devices`, `--ua-spoof`, `--delay`, etc.
- `loadConfigFile()` — Load optional `.browsercompare.json` config file
- Main flow: Merges defaults > config file > CLI args → calls `captureScreenshots()` → calls `generateReport()`

**CLI Options Exposed:**
```
--output, -o              Output directory (default: ./report)
--width, -w               Custom viewport width
--height, -h              Custom viewport height
--full-page               Capture full scrollable page (default: true)
--delay                   Wait ms after page load (default: 2000ms)
--devices                 Comma-separated device names
--browsers                Comma-separated engines: chromium,webkit,firefox
--ua-spoof                In-app browser UA spoofing
--inject-eruda            Inject Eruda debug console (optional)
```

---

#### 2. **lib/browsers.mjs** (105 lines) — Profile Definitions
**Purpose:** Define browser engines, devices, and in-app browser user agent strings

**Key Exports:**

**IN_APP_UA_STRINGS** — 10 in-app browser profiles:
```javascript
{
  facebook_ios, facebook_android,
  instagram_ios, instagram_android,
  tiktok_ios, tiktok_android,
  wechat, line, twitter_ios, snapchat
}
```
Each profile specifies:
- Real user agent string (from actual in-app browsers)
- Which engine to use (webkit for iOS, chromium for Android)
- Default device (iPhone 15 Pro or Pixel 7)
- Platform classification

**DEFAULT_DEVICES** — 9 device presets:
```
iPhone 15 Pro, iPhone 13, iPhone SE (3rd gen)
Pixel 7, Galaxy S9+
iPad Pro 11
Desktop Chrome, Desktop Safari, Desktop Firefox
```

**BROWSER_LAUNCHERS** — Maps browser names to Playwright launchers:
```javascript
{ chromium, webkit, firefox } → launcher objects
```

**Utility:** `getPlatform(device)` — Returns 'ios', 'android', or 'desktop'

---

#### 3. **lib/capture-engine.mjs** (200 lines) — Core Screenshot Capture
**Purpose:** Launch browsers, navigate to URLs, capture screenshots with retry logic

**Key Functions:**

**captureWithRetry(fn, label)** — Exponential backoff retry mechanism
- 3 max retries with 1s, 2s, 4s delays
- Returns `{ success: true, ...result }` or `{ success: false, error }`

**navigateAndSettle(page, url, delay)** — Two-phase page load strategy
1. Wait for `domcontentloaded` (reliable baseline)
2. Try to wait for `networkidle` with 5s timeout (best-effort, fails gracefully on analytics-heavy pages)
3. Wait additional configurable delay (default 2s)

**captureScreenshots(config)** — Main capture loop
1. **Standard browser combos:** For each browserName + deviceName:
   - Launch browser engine (chromium/webkit/firefox)
   - Skip incompatible combos (webkit ≠ android, firefox ≠ mobile)
   - Create context with device viewport, pixel ratio, UA
   - Navigate to URL and settle
   - Screenshot with `fullPage: true` (or viewport only)
   - Save to `screenshots/[engine]_[device].png`
   - Collect result metadata

2. **In-app browser UA spoofing:** For each requested UA spoof:
   - Match to IN_APP_UA_STRINGS profiles
   - Launch the matching engine (webkit or chromium)
   - Create context with spoofed UA string
   - Navigate, screenshot, save as `inapp_[key].png`

**Returns:** Array of screenshot results with metadata:
```javascript
[
  {
    browser: "Chromium (Chrome)",
    browserEngine: "chromium",
    device: "iPhone 15 Pro",
    viewport: { width, height },
    userAgent: "...",
    filename: "...",
    filepath: "...",
    type: "standard" | "in-app",
    platform: "ios" | "android" | "desktop"
  },
  ...
]
```

---

#### 4. **lib/report-generator.mjs** (102 lines) — HTML Report Generation
**Purpose:** Create the interactive HTML comparison report

**Key Functions:**

**generateReport(results, config, templateDir)** — Main generator
1. Creates `metadata.json` with capture results + timestamp
2. Reads `report-template.html` or generates minimal fallback
3. Injects metadata and URL into template as JSON
4. Writes final `index.html` to output directory
5. Logs summary

**generateDefaultTemplate()** — Fallback minimal report (if template not found)
- Basic responsive grid layout
- Dark theme
- Screenshot cards with browser/device labels

---

#### 5. **report-template.html** (592 lines) — Interactive Report UI
**Purpose:** Self-contained HTML/CSS/JS report viewer (no build dependencies)

**Features Implemented:**

**Views:**
- **Grid View** — Responsive grid of all screenshots (3 columns on desktop)
- **Side-by-Side View** — Horizontal scroll for sequential comparison
- **Diff/Compare Mode** — Select exactly 2 screenshots side-by-side

**Filtering:**
- **By Engine:** All / Chromium / WebKit / Firefox / In-App
- **By Platform:** All / iOS / Android / Computer (desktop)

**Interactive Elements:**
- **Click-to-Zoom Lightbox** — Full-screen image inspection
- **Stats Dashboard** — Shows total screenshots, # engines, # devices, # in-app browsers
- **Tags on Cards** — Visual indicators for engine type and platform
- **Responsive Design** — Works on mobile, tablets, desktop

**Styling:**
- Dark theme with GitHub-like color palette
- CSS variables for colors, fonts, spacing
- Smooth transitions on hover/interactions
- Monospace font for metadata (JetBrains Mono)
- Sans-serif for content (DM Sans)

**JavaScript Logic:**
```
- getFilteredResults()    → Filter by current engine/platform
- renderStats()           → Display overview metrics
- createCard(result, index) → Build HTML for one screenshot
- render()                → Update DOM based on current view/filters
- toggleDiffSelect()      → Add/remove from diff selections
- openLightbox()          → Show full-size image
```

---

### DATA FLOW

```
User runs: node capture.mjs https://example.com --browsers chromium,webkit

1. capture.mjs
   └─ parseArgs() → { url, browsers: [...], ... }
   └─ loadConfigFile() → merge with .browsercompare.json if exists
   └─ captureScreenshots(config)

2. capture-engine.mjs
   ├─ For each browser (chromium, webkit):
   │  └─ For each device (iPhone 15 Pro, Pixel 7, iPad Pro 11, ...):
   │     └─ captureWithRetry()
   │        ├─ browser.launch()
   │        ├─ browser.newContext({ ...device, viewport, userAgent })
   │        ├─ navigateAndSettle(url, delay)
   │        ├─ page.screenshot({ path, fullPage: true })
   │        └─ Return: { browser, device, filename, ... }
   │
   └─ For each in-app UA spoof (facebook, instagram, tiktok, ...):
      └─ captureWithRetry()
         ├─ browser.launch() [webkit for iOS, chromium for Android]
         ├─ browser.newContext({ ...device, userAgent: inAppUaString })
         ├─ navigateAndSettle(url, delay)
         ├─ page.screenshot()
         └─ Return: { browser: "Facebook In-App (iOS)", type: "in-app", ... }

3. report-generator.mjs
   ├─ Create metadata.json { url, capturedAt, results: [...] }
   └─ Generate index.html
      ├─ Embed metadata as JSON
      ├─ Include complete CSS + JS
      └─ Build interactive report

4. User opens report/index.html in browser
   ├─ Parse embedded metadata
   ├─ Render grid of screenshots
   ├─ Allow filtering by engine/platform
   ├─ Allow view mode switching
   └─ Allow click-to-zoom
```

---

### KEY DESIGN PRINCIPLES

1. **Single Responsibility:** Each module has one job
   - browsers.mjs: profile definitions
   - capture-engine.mjs: screenshot capture
   - report-generator.mjs: HTML generation
   - report-template.html: UI/UX only

2. **Retry Logic:** Exponential backoff for flaky page loads (up to 3 attempts)

3. **Two-Phase Navigation:** `domcontentloaded` (reliable) + `networkidle` (best-effort)

4. **Self-Contained Report:** HTML report has no external dependencies (fonts from CDN only), works offline

5. **Flexible Device Mapping:** Skips invalid browser/device combos (e.g., webkit on Android is iOS-only)

6. **Platform Classification:** Automatically derives iOS/Android/desktop from Playwright device profiles

---

### GENERATED OUTPUT

```
report/
├── index.html                 # Self-contained interactive report
├── metadata.json             # Structured capture results
│   {
│     "url": "...",
│     "capturedAt": "2026-02-22T12:26:45.151Z",
│     "results": [
│       {
│         "browser": "Chromium (Chrome)",
│         "browserEngine": "chromium",
│         "device": "iPhone 15 Pro",
│         "viewport": { "width": 393, "height": 659 },
│         "userAgent": "...",
│         "filename": "chromium_iPhone_15_Pro.png",
│         "type": "standard",
│         "platform": "ios"
│       },
│       ...
│     ]
│   }
└── screenshots/
    ├── chromium_iPhone_15_Pro.png
    ├── webkit_iPhone_15_Pro.png
    ├── firefox_Desktop_Chrome.png
    ├── inapp_facebook_ios.png
    └── ... (one per browser/device/UA combo)
```

---

### EXAMPLE USAGE

```bash
# Capture with defaults (all browsers, 6 devices, no in-app browsers)
node capture.mjs https://example.com

# Compare specific browsers and devices only
node capture.mjs https://example.com \
  --browsers chromium,webkit \
  --devices "iPhone 15 Pro,Pixel 7,iPad Pro 11"

# Test in-app browsers (spoofs UA, uses appropriate engine per platform)
node capture.mjs https://example.com \
  --ua-spoof facebook,instagram,tiktok,wechat,line,twitter,snapchat

# Full comparison with custom delay
node capture.mjs https://example.com \
  --browsers chromium,webkit,firefox \
  --devices "iPhone 15 Pro,Pixel 7,Galaxy S9+,iPad Pro 11,Desktop Chrome" \
  --ua-spoof facebook,instagram,tiktok \
  --delay 3000 \
  --output ./my-report

# Custom viewport
node capture.mjs https://example.com --width 390 --height 844
```

---

### TECHNICAL STACK

- **Node.js** (ES modules) — Runtime
- **Playwright v1.50.0** — Real browser engines (Chromium, WebKit, Firefox)
- **Plain HTML/CSS/JavaScript** — Report UI (no frameworks, no build step)

---

### DEPENDENCIES & FILE SIZES

- `capture.mjs`: 115 lines — CLI orchestration
- `lib/browsers.mjs`: 105 lines — Profile data
- `lib/capture-engine.mjs`: 200 lines — Capture logic with retry
- `lib/report-generator.mjs`: 102 lines — Report generation
- `report-template.html`: 592 lines — Complete interactive report
- **Total source code:** 1,114 lines (excluding node_modules)

---

### SUPPORTED IN-APP BROWSERS

| Platform | iOS Engine | Android Engine |
|----------|-----------|----------------|
| Facebook | WebKit (WKWebView) | Chromium (WebView) |
| Instagram | WebKit (WKWebView) | Chromium (WebView) |
| TikTok | WebKit (WKWebView) | Chromium (WebView) |
| WeChat | WebKit (WKWebView) | Tencent X5 Engine |
| LINE | WebKit (WKWebView) | Chromium (WebView) |
| Twitter/X | WebKit (WKWebView) | Chromium (WebView) |
| Snapchat | WebKit (WKWebView) | N/A |

---

### GENERATED REPORTS (Evidence in Repo)

The repo contains 4 example reports capturing real product pages:
- `report-tyvok-p1/` — Handheld laser engraver product page
- `report-keyirobot-loona/` — Robot product page
- `report-olanglab/` — SaaS application
- `report-olanglab-sonic/` — Variant of SaaS app

Each contains ~20 screenshots (3 browsers × 6-9 devices + in-app UA variants).

---

### WHAT'S BUILT vs. WHAT'S TODO

✅ **Fully Implemented:**
- Core capture logic with Playwright
- Retry mechanism with exponential backoff
- 10 in-app browser UA profiles with correct engines
- 9 device presets
- Full interactive HTML report with grid/side-by-side/diff views
- Filter by browser engine and platform
- Click-to-zoom lightbox
- Stats dashboard
- Responsive design
- Config file support (.browsercompare.json)

🔧 **Not Yet Implemented (from CONTEXT.md):**
- Pixel-diff highlighting (CSS diff visualization)
- Automated feature detection tests (injected JS checks)
- Watch mode for development
- CI/CD integration (JSON output for pipelines)
- Interactive test page with known bugs (100vh, sticky, etc.)

---

This is a **production-ready CLI tool** with a clean, modular architecture following SOLID principles. The codebase is minimal (~1,100 lines) yet feature-complete for the core use case: capturing and comparing browser rendering across devices.

node capture.mjs https://example.com --width 390 --height 844
```

---

### TECHNICAL STACK

- **Node.js** (ES modules) — Runtime
- **Playwright v1.50.0** — Real browser engines (Chromium, WebKit, Firefox)
- **Plain HTML/CSS/JavaScript** — Report UI (no frameworks, no build step)

---

### DEPENDENCIES & FILE SIZES

- `capture.mjs`: 115 lines — CLI orchestration
- `lib/browsers.mjs`: 105 lines — Profile data
- `lib/capture-engine.mjs`: 200 lines — Capture logic with retry
- `lib/report-generator.mjs`: 102 lines — Report generation
- `report-template.html`: 592 lines — Complete interactive report
- **Total source code:** 1,114 lines (excluding node_modules)

---

### SUPPORTED IN-APP BROWSERS

| Platform | iOS Engine | Android Engine |
|----------|-----------|----------------|
| Facebook | WebKit (WKWebView) | Chromium (WebView) |
| Instagram | WebKit (WKWebView) | Chromium (WebView) |
| TikTok | WebKit (WKWebView) | Chromium (WebView) |
| WeChat | WebKit (WKWebView) | Tencent X5 Engine |
| LINE | WebKit (WKWebView) | Chromium (WebView) |
| Twitter/X | WebKit (WKWebView) | Chromium (WebView) |
| Snapchat | WebKit (WKWebView) | N/A |

---

### GENERATED REPORTS (Evidence in Repo)

The repo contains 4 example reports capturing real product pages:
- `report-tyvok-p1/` — Handheld laser engraver product page
- `report-keyirobot-loona/` — Robot product page
- `report-olanglab/` — SaaS application
- `report-olanglab-sonic/` — Variant of SaaS app

Each contains ~20 screenshots (3 browsers × 6-9 devices + in-app UA variants).

---

### WHAT'S BUILT vs. WHAT'S TODO

✅ **Fully Implemented:**
- Core capture logic with Playwright
- Retry mechanism with exponential backoff
- 10 in-app browser UA profiles with correct engines
- 9 device presets
- Full interactive HTML report with grid/side-by-side/diff views
- Filter by browser engine and platform
- Click-to-zoom lightbox
- Stats dashboard
- Responsive design
- Config file support (.browsercompare.json)

🔧 **Not Yet Implemented (from CONTEXT.md):**
- Pixel-diff highlighting (CSS diff visualization)
- Automated feature detection tests (injected JS checks)
- Watch mode for development
- CI/CD integration (JSON output for pipelines)
- Interactive test page with known bugs (100vh, sticky, etc.)

---

This is a **production-ready CLI tool** with a clean, modular architecture following SOLID principles. The codebase is minimal (~1,100 lines) yet feature-complete for the core use case: capturing and comparing browser rendering across devices.

Here are the bugs I found, organized by severity. --- ## Critical ### 1. Browser leaked if capture throws before `browser.close()` — in-app loop **File:** `/Users/kenliu/Code/browser-comp/lib/capture-engine.mjs`, lines 159 and 195 **Confidence: 90** In the in-app UA spoofing loop, `browser.close()` is called at line 195 after `captureWithRetry` completes. However, `captureWithRetry` itself calls `context.close()` inside the retry callback (line 175), but if `browserConfig.launcher.launch()` (line 159) succeeds and then `captureWithRetry` throws an unhandled error escaping its own try/catch, the browser will never be closed. More concretely: this browser is launched fresh per key *inside* the inner loop (line 159), and only closed at line 195. If any non-captured error propagates out of `captureWithRetry`, the outer `for (const spoofKey of uaSpoof)` loop crashes, leaving the browser process running. The same structural issue exists in the outer standard-browser loop (line 80/139), but there, the browser is shared across all devices for a given engine, which is the intended design — the exposure is the same. Fix: wrap browser launch and use in a try/finally block: ```js const browser = await browserConfig.launcher.launch({ headless: true }); try { // ... captureWithRetry ... } finally { await browser.close(); } ``` --- ## Important ### 2. `metadata.results.indexOf(r)` returns `-1` for duplicate objects **File:** `/Users/kenliu/Code/browser-comp/report-template.html`, line 521 **Confidence: 85** ```js container.innerHTML = filtered.map((r, i) => createCard(r, metadata.results.indexOf(r))).join(''); ``` `filtered` is produced by `Array.filter()` on `metadata.results`, so each element in `filtered` *is* the same object reference as the one in `metadata.results`. Under normal operation this works. However, `indexOf` uses reference equality. The issue is **100% real and verified**.

**Verification:**

1. **Line 40 in report-generator.mjs** uses `JSON.stringify(metadata)` which does NOT escape HTML special characters like `</script>`

JSON.stringify does not escape `</script>` sequences, and the metadata object containing user-controlled URLs is directly embedded in an inline script block without any escaping, enabling script injection if a URL contains `</script>`.

content.text: --- ### Code Review Found 7 issues: --- **1. Context leaked on every failed retry** (bug) `captureWithRetry` retries the entire callback, but `context.close()` is only called on the happy path. If `navigateAndSettle` or `page.screenshot` throws, the context is abandoned. With `MAX_RETRIES=3`, up to 2 contexts leak per device per browser before giving up. Affects both the standard capture loop and the in-app UA loop. https://github.com/vkenliu/browser-compatibility/blob/f93f5e0983bcc0320c4df5164e6eac9af433cca1/lib/capture-engine.mjs#L99-L132 Fix: wrap the callback body in `try/finally { await context.close(); }`. --- **2. Browser process leaked if device loop throws** (bug) `browser.close()` is called unconditionally after the device loop but has no `try/finally` guard. If `browser.newContext()` throws (e.g., browser process crash), the exception escapes the loop and `browser.close()` is never reached, orphaning the OS process. https://github.com/vkenliu/browser-compatibility/blob/f93f5e0983bcc0320c4df5164e6eac9af433cca1/lib/capture-engine.mjs#L79-L140 --- **3. XSS: `config.url` injected unescaped into HTML** (security bug) `config.url` from CLI input is substituted directly into the generated HTML without HTML-encoding. A URL like `https://x.com` breaks out of the `` element and executes JS in anyone who opens the report. https://github.com/vkenliu/browser-compatibility/blob/f93f5e0983bcc0320c4df5164e6eac9af433cca1/lib/report-generator.mjs#L38-L43 Fix: HTML-encode `config.url` before substitution. --- **4. XSS: `result.*` fields interpolated unescaped into `innerHTML`** (security bug) `createCard()` interpolates `result.browser`, `result.device`, `result.browserEngine`, and `result.filename` directly into a template literal assigned to `innerHTML`. `result.device` flows from the `--devices` CLI flag with no sanitization — passing `--devices ''` executes JS in the report. https://github.com/vkenliu/browser-compatibility/blob/f93f5e0983bcc0320c4df5164e6eac9af433cca1/report-template.html#L484-L495 --- **5. `--inject-eruda` flag is silently non-functional** (bug) Eruda is injected into the initial `about:blank` page before `navigateAndSettle` is called. The subsequent `page.goto()` navigates away, destroying the blank page context and everything injected into it. The flag has no effect on the target page. https://github.com/vkenliu/browser-compatibility/blob/f93f5e0983bcc0320c4df5164e6eac9af433cca1/lib/capture-engine.mjs#L106-L113 Fix: move the Eruda injection to after `navigateAndSettle`. --- **6. Diff view renders all filtered cards instead of only the 2 selected ones** (bug) `render()` always passes every filtered result to `createCard()` regardless of `currentView`. In diff mode, all matching cards appear in the 2-column grid instead of just the 2 selected screenshots. `diffSelections` is tracked but never consulted in `render()`. https://github.com/vkenliu/browser-compatibility/blob/f93f5e0983bcc0320c4df5164e6eac9af433cca1/report-template.html#L519-L522 --- **7. `diff-selected` CSS class is never defined — no visual selection feedback** (bug) `createCard()` applies `class="card diff-selected"` to selected diff cards, but no `.diff-selected` rule exists anywhere in the stylesheet. Users get no visual indication of which cards they've picked for comparison, making the diff workflow unusable. https://github.com/vkenliu/browser-compatibility/blob/f93f5e0983bcc0320c4df5164e6eac9af433cca1/report-template.html#L479-L481 ---
